L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states conditions were 'terrible,' with strong sustained gusts and flags horizontal, and that two companions decided to walk down after waiting because conditions only worsened. Despite being told it was not worth it, he chose to jump, and after an on-heading opening a gust immediately pushed him back into the wall, producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrouted bridle setup
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The fatal mechanism was a pilot chute in tow caused by the bridle being routed on the right leg strap, which locked the bridle and prevented canopy extraction. The narrative states the pilot chute inflated normally after a stable 8-second deployment, but the container remained closed because of this rigging error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in unsafe winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states winds were 20 knots sustained with gusts well over 30 from the left, and the other four experienced jumpers in the group decided conditions were unsafe and stood down. Renzo then launched into that strong crosswind gust, and the wind immediately pushed him sideways and back toward the wall, making the decision to jump in those conditions a direct producing factor.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy during instability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit he went from stable to head-low within the first second, tumbled along the face for three or four seconds, and never got a hand to his pilot chute. The narrative explicitly states he failed to prioritize deployment over recovering stability, and this failure to deploy directly produced the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After an unstable exit, he spent the entire freefall trying to recover to belly instead of pitching the pilot chute. He impacted about 6 seconds after exit with the pilot chute still in the BOC, so the direct fatal mechanism was failure to deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking suit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit, he was unable to control the tracking suit effectively, 'could not get forward drive,' and descended on a steep angle until impacting a ledge about 7 seconds after exit. The narrative explicitly states the problem was not the exit choice but his inability to fly the suit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Asymmetric toggle input
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 180-degree off-heading toward the building, she went straight for her toggles, found only the right one, and pulled it hard. That control input drove the canopy into a diving turn directly into the glass facade, producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in zero visibility
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited despite fog reducing visibility to essentially zero, with the fjord, landing area, and even the wall below the edge not visible. This made terrain clearance by visual reference impossible, and he tracked into an unseen protruding ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stopped tracking while distracted
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the flight, the jumper appeared to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro. While doing so, he stopped tracking, flattened his body position, drifted left toward the wall, made no corrective input, and impacted a ledge in freefall without deploying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight despite known stall issue
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he had at least four prior near-miss stalls from excessively high angle of attack, recognized the problem as serious, and said he should stop cliff flying and return to the dropzone for 50 training skydives before doing more cliff flights. He nevertheless continued wingsuit cliff jumping every weekend, and on this jump again stalled the suit and impacted terrain in freefall with no deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 90-degree off-heading opening toward guy wires approximately 40 feet away, multiple witnesses stated he made no toggle or riser input and kept his arms at his sides. The fatal outcome was produced by his failure to execute an immediate canopy heading correction before striking the wires.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped damaged pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After being told the pilot chute needed replacement and being offered a spare, the jumper chose to use a pilot chute that had been torn on the previous jump. The narrative states the damaged pilot chute fluttered, could not hold air, and remained ineffective, resulting in impact with a closed container.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed airspace check before deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states that David tracked directly over the second jumper and pitched into the second jumper's inflating canopy. It explicitly says David did not check his airspace before deploying, and this deployment into occupied airspace led to the canopy entanglement and fatal fall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Intentionally excessive delay
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he had a clean exit, good track, no wind issue, no gear issue, and no instability, but he deliberately delayed deployment until just above the water. He was known for progressively pushing delays longer despite repeated warnings, and on this jump chose a delay that left insufficient altitude for canopy inflation, producing the fatal low-pull impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit stall in flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states that about halfway through the flight his angle of attack shifted, he appeared to stall the suit, and his glide changed from shallow to nearly vertical within about two seconds. This loss of aerodynamic performance directly produced the unrecovered sink and terminal freefall impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable tracking exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states a possible unstable exit and identifies not enough forward separation on exit. At this object, previous jumpers reported being very close to the cliff after 4 seconds, so inadequate execution of the exit/initial track is the best-supported active failure producing the near-exit impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delay/opening sequence unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The report also suggests the jumper may have taken too long a delay and either impacted in freefall or opened close to the wall with an off-heading. Human error is evident, but the narrative does not provide enough detail to determine whether the fatal mechanism was an excessive delay decision, a low-separation opening, or freefall impact before effective canopy flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrouted pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The fatal mechanism was a packing/setup execution error: the pilot chute was packed inside the closed main container instead of being routed to the BOC pouch. When he pulled the BOC handle, it detached cleanly with no pilot chute attached, producing a pilot-chute-in-tow/closed-container total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he 'pulled very late and impacted at line stretch,' making the fatal mechanism a failure to deploy at a survivable altitude. The report is clear that the late pull caused the death, even though the reason for the delay cannot be determined from available evidence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unresolved canopy malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video evidence shows a stable exit, a 3-second deployment, clean bag extraction, and then about 8 seconds of the jumper violently kicking and working the risers until impact. A human error is implicated because the malfunction was not resolved, but the canopy is never visible, so the malfunction type and whether the jumper's response was appropriate cannot be determined from the narrative.
